questions.title: Short Answer

parts.desc: Speed derivation using conservation of energy
Free body diagram at top of loop

questions.answer:
Part (a) — 2 pts
Draw energy bars whose total heights add to 6 units; Ug bar at height 2 units, K bar at height 4 units.

Part (b) — 2 pts
Use conservation of energy: Ei = Ef
Mg(6R) = Mg(2R) + ½Mv²
4gR = ½v² → v = √(8gR)

Part (c)(i) — 2 pts
At Point C (top of loop): draw a downward arrow labeled Fg (gravitational force) and a downward arrow labeled FN (normal force). Both forces point toward the center of the loop.

Part (c)(ii) — 1 pt
If released from height 4R, by conservation of energy the block would have zero speed at Point C. With zero speed, the block cannot maintain contact with the loop and will fall. Therefore the claim is incorrect.

questions.title: Experimental Design

parts.desc: Design experiment to determine spring constant k
Change in momentum from force-time graph
Confirm momentum change with velocity-time graph

questions.answer:
Part (a) — 4 pts
Procedure: Measure mass with digital scale. Hang cylinder from spring, pull down and release to set into oscillatory motion. Use stopwatch to measure period of multiple oscillations. Repeat with different masses to reduce uncertainty.

Part (b)(i) — 1 pt
Plot m vs. T² (mass vs. period squared) to get a linear graph. Slope = k/(4π²).

Part (b)(ii) — 1 pt
k = (slope) × 4π²

Part (c)(i) — 3 pts
ΔK = Kf − Ki = ½mvf² − ½mvi²
= ½(0.25)(0)² − ½(0.25)(0.3)²
ΔK ≈ −0.0113 J

Part (c)(ii) — 2 pts
The area under the force-time graph from t = 0.5 s to t = 2.5 s represents the impulse (change in momentum). The area is approximately zero.

Part (c)(iii) — 1 pt
The velocity-time graph shows velocity is 0.3 m/s at both t = 0.5 s and t = 2.5 s. Since p = mv, momentum is the same at both times, confirming Δp = 0.

questions.title: Qualitative/Quantitative Translation
questions.answer:
Part (a) — 3 pts
Free body diagram: gravitational force Fg downward at center, tension FT upward-left at right end, hinge force FH at left end. Three vectors must form an equilibrium system.

Part (b) — 2 pts
FT2 > FT1. When the angle decreases, the perpendicular component of tension must remain the same to balance the torque. A smaller angle means larger total tension is needed.

Part (c) — 3 pts
Στ = 0 (equilibrium)
(Fg)(L/2)(sin 90°) − FT(L)(sin θ) = 0
Mg(L/2) = FT(L)(sin θ)
FT = Mg / (2 sin θ)

Part (d) — 2 pts
The equation shows FT is inversely proportional to sin θ. For θ < 90°, as θ decreases, sin θ decreases, so FT increases. This confirms the reasoning in part (b).

Part (e) — 2 pts
Draw a graph of angular speed vs. time that is monotonically increasing and concave down (starts steep, levels off).

questions.title: Paragraph Argument

parts.desc: Compare work done on different planets

questions.answer:
Part (a) — 2 pts
On Planet X (larger radius, same mass), gravitational force is smaller (F = GMm/R²). Since the sphere travels the same vertical distance on both planets, and work = force × distance, less work is done on Planet X.

Part (b) — 5 pts
T = 2π√(L/g). On Planet Y, the gravitational force is larger (larger mass), so g is larger. A larger g means a smaller period. However, the increased gravitational force could stretch the string, increasing the pendulum length L. Since T increases with L, a longer string could lead to a larger period. Therefore, the effect on the period depends on whether the change in g or the change in L dominates.
